May 6, 1983 
Re: 
Your Request for Written 
Advice on Behalf of Mayor Rey 
Retzlaff; Our File 
No. A-83-104 
Your letter asks for our advice regarding a decision 
involving tne Scotts Valley Drive Storm Drain Basin and 
Improvement District. 
You have stated tne facts as follows: 
FACTS 
1. 
Sometime on or about February 1982, tne Scotts Valley 
City Council adopted resolutions set;ting forth the proposed 
boundaries of the above-referenced assessment district. 
2. 
Should the assessment district proceed to completion, 
public improvements that would be financed and constructed would 
consist of such tnlngs as storm drains, curbs and gutters, 
street lignting, and street widening_ 
3. 
Mayor Retzlaff does not own any property within the 
boundaries of the proposed assessment district. 
4. 
However, certain property commonly known as the "Hot Dog 
Stand" property is owned by his children and their spouses. 
None of the owners of the property reside within the Ci 
of 
Scotts Valley. 
s. 
Mayor Retzlaff's children (and their spouses) who own 
the aforesaid property, collectively owe him a debt in excess of 
$1,0. 
is 
is in the form 
an unsecured note. 
Retzlaff has no rights or interest in or to tne "Hot Dog S 
" 
pr 
• 
note requ 
no monthly 
, the sum owing 
is merely Que and payable at some date in the future. 


Raymond M. Haignt 
May 6, 1983 
Page 2 
6. 
The property in question is worth something in excess of 
$250,000. 
It is currently rented to a tenant. 
You do not know 
the monthly rental amount, but believe it to be in excess of 
$300 per month. 
7. 
It appears that this property would be materially 
affected by the proposed assessment district in that it would 
undoubtedly bear substantial assessments once they are levied. 
QUESTION PRESENTED 
The question presented for our review and advice is as 
follows: 
On the basis of the foregoing fact situation, would Mayor 
Retzlaff have a conflict of interest with respect to 
participating in decisions such as confirmation and spread of 
levy of assessments, confirmation of engineer's report, approval 
of design and construction plans, change of assessment district 
boundaries, and the like? 
ANALYSIS 
The Political Reform Actll requires that public officials 
not participate in governmental decisions in which they have a 
financial interest. 
Section 87100. 
Financial interest is 
defined in Section 87103 to lnclude certain investments, certain 
interests in real property, sources of income and employers and 
business entities where the official is an officer or director. 
In our previous advice letter (Advice Letter to Raymond M. 
Haight, No. A-81-S09, Oct. 16, 1981) we stated that Mr. Retzlaff 
had no interest in the real property in question since he had 
sold it to his children and had taKen in return only an 
unsecured note. 
However, that conclusion did not take into 
account the provisions of Civil Code Section 3046, of which we 
have since become appraised. 
Civil Code Section 3046 provides 
as follows: 
One who sells real property has a vendor's lien 
thereon, independent of possession, for so much of the 
price as remains unpaid and unsecured otherwise than by 
the personal obligatlon of the buyer. 
II Government Code Sections 81000-91014. 
All statutory 
references are to the C~vernment Code unless otherwise specified. 


Raymond M. Haight 
May 6, 1983 
Page 3 
The effect of this section is to continue in Mr. Retzlaff a 
beneficial interest~/ in the "Hot Dog Stand" property in the 
form of a vendor's lien. 
The statute creates a form of security 
interest in the property where none is otherwise taken. 
An interest in real property wortn more than $1,000 can form 
the basis for a conflict of interest.l/ 
Your letter indicates 
that the "Hot Dog Stand" property will be materially affected by 
the decisions. 
As we held previously,i/ effects upon it will 
be distinguishable from effects on the public generally. 
Therefore, Mr. Retzlaff has a conflict of interest as to the 
storm draln basin decisions requiring nis disqualification. 
If you have any furtner questions regarding this letter, 
please do not hesitate to contact me. 
I may be reached at 
916/322-5901. 
REL: km 
Sincerely, 
Robert E. Leidigh 
Counsel, Legal Division 
~/ Section 82033 defines an "interest in real property" 
as including any beneficial interest. 
1/ Section 87103(b) • 
!/ Acvice letter to Raymond M. Haight, No. A-81-509, 
October 16, 1981, at pages 8-10. 


Robert E. Leidigh 
Counsel 
Legal Division 
RAYMOND M. HAIGHT 
ATTORN EY AT LAW 
4740 SCOTTS VALLEY DRlVE. SUlTE A 
POBOX 66659 
SCOITS VALLEY, CALIFORNIA 95066 
(408) 438-66l0 
April 11, 1983 
Fair Political Practi.ces Commission 
P.O. Box 807 
Sacramento, California 
95804 
Re: 
Request for Written Advice 
Regarding Scotts Valley Drive Storm 
Drain Basin and Improvement District -
Retzlaff 
Dear Mr. Leidigh: 
Mayor Rey Retzlaff of the Scotts Valley City council 
has requested that I seek your written advice regarding the 
following fact 
situation, involving the Scotts Valley Drive 
Storm Drain Basin and Improvement District: 
FACTS 
1. 
Sometime on or about February, 1982, the Scotts 
Valley City Council adopted resolutions setting forth the 
proposed boundaries of the above-referenced assessment district. 
2. 
Should the assessment district proceed to completion 
public improvements that would be financed and constructed 
would consist of such things as storm drains, curbs and gutters, 
street lighting, and street widening. 
3. 
Mayor Retzlaff does not own any property within 
the boundaries of the proposed assessment district. 
4. 
However, certain property commonly known as the 
"Hot Dog Stand" property is owned by his children and their 
spouses. 
None of the owners of the property reside within 
the City of Scotts Valley. 
5. 
Mayor Retzlaff's children (and their spouses) 
who own the aforesaid property, collectively owe him a debt 
in excess of $1,000.00. 
This debt is in the form of an un-
secured note. 
Mayor Retzlaff has no rights or interest in 
or to the tlHot Dog Stand" property. 


Robert E. Leidigh 
April 11, 1983 
PAGE TWO 
6. 
The property in question is worth something in 
excess of $250,000.00. 
It is currently rented to a tenant. 
I do not know the monthly rental amount, but I believe it 
to be in excess of $300.00 per month. 
7. 
It appears that this property would be materially 
affected by the proposed assessment district in that it would 
undoubtedly bear substantial assessments once they are levied. 
QUESTION PRESENTED 
The question presented for your review and advice 
is as follows: 
On the basis 
of the foregoing fact situation, would 
Mayor Retzlaff have a conflict of interest with respect to 
participating in decisions such as confirmation and spread 
of levy of assessments, confirmation of engineer's report, 
approval of design and construction plans, change of assess-
ment district boundaries, and the like? 
If you need further clairification of the fact situation 
please contact me. 
Thank you ln advance for your courtesy and cooperation 
in this matter. 
Very truly yours, 
RMH/pam

Mr. Bob Rocket,

sections.question_synthetic: Does Mayor Retzlaff have a conflict of interest that would disqualify him from participating in decisions regarding a proposed assessment district when his children own property that would be materially affected by the district, and he holds an unsecured promissory note from them exceeding $1,000?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Yes, Mayor Retzlaff has a conflict of interest and must be disqualified from participating in storm drain basin decisions. Although he does not directly own the affected property, California Civil Code Section 3046 creates a vendor's lien in his favor on the 'Hot Dog Stand' property, constituting a beneficial interest in real property exceeding $1,000 that will be materially affected by the assessment district decisions.
sections.parsing_notes: Mixed formats: modern, old; LLM: Document includes both the incoming request letter (April 11, 1983) and FPPC response letter (May 6, 1983). Analysis section clearly discusses statutory basis (Section 87100, 87103, Civil Code 3046) and references prior advice letter A-81-509. Conclusion is explicit and unambiguous.

QUESTION: The question presented for our review and advice is as 
follows: 
On the basis of the foregoing fact situation, would Mayor 
Retzlaff have a conflict of interest with respect to 
participating in decisions such as confirmation and spread of 
levy of assessments, confirmation of engineer's report, approval 
of design and construction plans, change of assessment district 
boundaries, and the like?

CONCLUSION: Therefore, Mr. Retzlaff has a conflict of interest as to the storm drain basin decisions requiring his disqualification.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter responding to City Attorney Haight's request regarding whether Mayor Rey Retzlaff has a conflict of interest in decisions affecting a proposed storm drain assessment district. The FPPC concludes that Retzlaff must recuse himself because his unsecured promissory note from his children creates a vendor's lien under Civil Code Section 3046, giving him a beneficial interest in their $250,000+ property that will be materially affected by the assessment district.
